items.title: Request to Enable Section 270AA Immunity Applications for Misreporting Cases
items.summary: A representation seeks activation of Form 68 filing for misreporting cases after the Finance Act, 2026 expanded immunity under Section 270AA. The post Request to Enable Section 270AA Immunity Applications for Misreporting Cases appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, has undergone numerous amendments over the years to refine its provisions and ensure better compliance. One such provision is Section 270AA, which provides immunity from penalty and prosecution to taxpayers who have misreported their income. This section was introduced to encourage taxpayers to come clean about their misreported income and pay the requisite tax, interest, and penalty. The Finance Act, 2026, has further expanded the scope of this immunity, making it applicable to a wider range of cases. To avail of this immunity, taxpayers are required to file Form 68, which is a crucial document for seeking immunity under Section 270AA.

Section 270AA of the Income Tax Act, 1961, is a significant provision that provides relief to taxpayers who have made errors in their tax returns. The section states that if a taxpayer has misreported their income, they can apply for immunity from penalty and prosecution by filing Form 68 and paying the requisite tax, interest, and penalty. This provision is designed to encourage taxpayers to come forward and disclose their true income, thereby increasing tax compliance and revenue collection. The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has the authority to grant immunity under this section, and the process is governed by the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the rules made thereunder.

The expansion of immunity under Section 270AA by the Finance Act, 2026, is a welcome move, as it provides an opportunity for taxpayers to rectify their mistakes and avoid penal consequences. The CBDT has been empowered to grant immunity in cases where the taxpayer has made a genuine mistake or has been unable to report their income accurately due to circumstances beyond their control. This move is expected to boost tax compliance and reduce litigation, as taxpayers will be encouraged to come forward and disclose their true income without fear of penal consequences.

The Development in Detail

A recent representation has been made to the CBDT to activate the filing of Form 68 for misreporting cases, in light of the expanded immunity under Section 270AA. This representation highlights the need for a clear and streamlined process for taxpayers to apply for immunity under this section. The representation also emphasizes the importance of providing taxpayers with a fair opportunity to rectify their mistakes and avoid penal consequences. The CBDT is expected to consider this representation and take necessary steps to facilitate the filing of Form 68 and the grant of immunity under Section 270AA.

The development is significant, as it highlights the importance of providing taxpayers with a fair and transparent process for seeking immunity under Section 270AA. The representation made to the CBDT is a timely reminder of the need for a clear and streamlined process, which will encourage taxpayers to come forward and disclose their true income. The CBDT's response to this representation will be closely watched, as it will have a significant impact on tax compliance and revenue collection. The authority is expected to take a balanced approach, ensuring that taxpayers are provided with a fair opportunity to seek immunity, while also preventing abuse of this provision.

The activation of Form 68 filing for misreporting cases will have a significant impact on taxpayers who have made errors in their tax returns. Taxpayers who have misreported their income will now have an opportunity to rectify their mistakes and avoid penal consequences. This move is expected to boost tax compliance, as taxpayers will be encouraged to come forward and disclose their true income. The CBDT's decision on this matter will be closely watched, as it will have a significant impact on the tax landscape in India.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision of Section 270AA is the grant of immunity from penalty and prosecution to taxpayers who have misreported their income. This provision is significant, as it provides relief to taxpayers who have made errors in their tax returns. The section states that if a taxpayer has misreported their income, they can apply for immunity by filing Form 68 and paying the requisite tax, interest, and penalty. The CBDT has the authority to grant immunity under this section, and the process is governed by the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the rules made thereunder.

The impact of the expanded immunity under Section 270AA will be significant, as it will provide taxpayers with a fair opportunity to rectify their mistakes and avoid penal consequences. The provision is expected to boost tax compliance, as taxpayers will be encouraged to come forward and disclose their true income. The CBDT's decision to activate the filing of Form 68 for misreporting cases will further facilitate this process, providing taxpayers with a clear and streamlined process for seeking immunity. The move is expected to reduce litigation, as taxpayers will be encouraged to come forward and disclose their true income, rather than risking penal consequences.

The provision of Section 270AA is also significant, as it highlights the importance of providing taxpayers with a fair and transparent process for seeking immunity. The section states that the CBDT shall grant immunity to a taxpayer who has made a genuine mistake or has been unable to report their income accurately due to circumstances beyond their control. This provision is designed to prevent abuse of the immunity provision, while also providing taxpayers with a fair opportunity to rectify their mistakes. The CBDT's decision on this matter will be closely watched, as it will have a significant impact on tax compliance and revenue collection.

Critical Analysis

The expansion of immunity under Section 270AA is a welcome move, as it provides taxpayers with a fair opportunity to rectify their mistakes and avoid penal consequences. However, the provision also raises several critical issues, which need to be addressed by the CBDT. One of the key issues is the definition of "misreporting" and the circumstances under which a taxpayer can seek immunity. The section states that a taxpayer can seek immunity if they have made a genuine mistake or have been unable to report their income accurately due to circumstances beyond their control.

The CBDT needs to provide clarity on the definition of "misreporting" and the circumstances under which a taxpayer can seek immunity. This clarity is essential, as it will help taxpayers understand their rights and obligations under the provision. The authority also needs to ensure that the process for seeking immunity is fair and transparent, and that taxpayers are provided with a clear and streamlined process for filing Form 68. The CBDT's decision on this matter will be closely watched, as it will have a significant impact on tax compliance and revenue collection.

The critical analysis of the provision also highlights the importance of preventing abuse of the immunity provision. The section states that the CBDT shall grant immunity to a taxpayer who has made a genuine mistake or has been unable to report their income accurately due to circumstances beyond their control. However, the authority needs to ensure that this provision is not misused by taxpayers who have intentionally misreported their income. The CBDT needs to strike a balance between providing taxpayers with a fair opportunity to rectify their mistakes and preventing abuse of the provision.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the expanded immunity under Section 270AA are significant, as taxpayers who have misreported their income will now have an opportunity to rectify their mistakes and avoid penal consequences. Taxpayers who have made errors in their tax returns should take immediate action to rectify their mistakes and file Form 68 to seek immunity. The following are some key action points for taxpayers and practitioners:

1. Review tax returns: Taxpayers should review their tax returns to identify any errors or misreporting of income. This review should be done carefully, as it will help taxpayers identify their mistakes and take corrective action.

2. File Form 68: Taxpayers who have misreported their income should file Form 68 to seek immunity under Section 270AA. This form should be filed carefully, as it will help taxpayers avoid penal consequences.

3. Pay tax, interest, and penalty: Taxpayers who have misreported their income should pay the requisite tax, interest, and penalty to seek immunity under Section 270AA. This payment should be made carefully, as it will help taxpayers avoid penal consequences.

4. Maintain documentation: Taxpayers should maintain proper documentation to support their claim for immunity under Section 270AA. This documentation should include records of income, expenses, and tax payments.

5. Seek professional advice: Taxpayers who have misreported their income should seek professional advice to ensure that they are taking the correct steps to rectify their mistakes and avoid penal consequences. This advice should be sought from a qualified tax professional, as it will help taxpayers navigate the complex tax laws and regulations.

items.title: Post-Death ITRs Require Financial Verification for Income Assessment: SC
items.summary: Supreme Court held post-death ITRs require financial verification, fixed annual income on available records, enhanced compensation, and allowed the appeal. The post Post-Death ITRs Require Financial Verification for Income Assessment: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, governs the taxation of income in India, and its provisions are applicable to all individuals, including those who have passed away. Section 159 of the Act deals with the income-tax liability of a deceased person, which is assessed in the hands of the legal representative of the deceased. The legal representative is required to file the income-tax return (ITR) on behalf of the deceased person, which is commonly known as a post-death ITR. The assessment of income in such cases requires careful consideration of the available records and financial information.

Section 144 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, empowers the Assessing Officer to make an assessment to the best of his judgment, in cases where the taxpayer has not filed a return or has filed an incomplete return. This provision is often invoked in post-death ITR cases, where the legal representative may not have access to all the financial records of the deceased. The Assessing Officer must, however, ensure that the assessment is fair and reasonable, based on the available information.

The concept of financial verification is crucial in post-death ITR cases, as it enables the Assessing Officer to arrive at a reasonable estimate of the deceased person's income. This involves analyzing the available financial records, such as bank statements, investment documents, and other relevant papers. The Assessing Officer must also consider the provisions of Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the method of accounting and the computation of income.

The Development in Detail

The recent Supreme Court judgment has clarified that post-death ITRs require financial verification for income assessment. The Court held that the Assessing Officer must fix the annual income on the basis of available records and enhance compensation accordingly. This judgment is significant, as it provides clarity on the procedure to be followed in post-death ITR cases. The Court's decision is based on the principles of natural justice and the need to ensure that the assessment is fair and reasonable.

The Supreme Court's judgment is a culmination of a long-drawn litigation process, which involved the interpretation of various provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961. The Court's decision is based on a careful analysis of the statutory provisions, as well as the facts and circumstances of the case. The judgment is expected to have a significant impact on the assessment of post-death ITRs, as it provides a clear framework for the Assessing Officer to follow.

The Court's decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of financial verification in post-death ITR cases. The Assessing Officer must ensure that the assessment is based on a thorough analysis of the available financial records, rather than relying on estimates or assumptions. This approach is consistent with the principles of taxation, which require that the assessment be fair, reasonable, and based on the actual income of the taxpayer.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Supreme Court's judgment has significant implications for post-death ITR cases, as it provides clarity on the procedure to be followed in such cases. The judgment emphasizes the importance of financial verification, which is a critical aspect of the assessment process. The Assessing Officer must ensure that the assessment is based on a thorough analysis of the available financial records, rather than relying on estimates or assumptions.

The judgment also highlights the need for the Assessing Officer to fix the annual income on the basis of available records and enhance compensation accordingly. This approach is consistent with the principles of taxation, which require that the assessment be fair, reasonable, and based on the actual income of the taxpayer. The judgment is expected to have a significant impact on the assessment of post-death ITRs, as it provides a clear framework for the Assessing Officer to follow.

The impact of the judgment will be felt by legal representatives, who will need to ensure that they provide the Assessing Officer with all the necessary financial records and information. The judgment also emphasizes the importance of maintaining accurate and complete financial records, which is essential for a fair and reasonable assessment. The provisions of Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the method of accounting and the computation of income, will also assume significance in post-death ITR cases.

Critical Analysis

The Supreme Court's judgment is a significant development in the field of taxation, as it provides clarity on the procedure to be followed in post-death ITR cases. The judgment highlights the importance of financial verification, which is a critical aspect of the assessment process. The Assessing Officer must ensure that the assessment is based on a thorough analysis of the available financial records, rather than relying on estimates or assumptions.

The judgment is also significant, as it emphasizes the need for the Assessing Officer to fix the annual income on the basis of available records and enhance compensation accordingly. This approach is consistent with the principles of taxation, which require that the assessment be fair, reasonable, and based on the actual income of the taxpayer. The judgment is expected to have a significant impact on the assessment of post-death ITRs, as it provides a clear framework for the Assessing Officer to follow.

The judgment will also have implications for legal representatives, who will need to ensure that they provide the Assessing Officer with all the necessary financial records and information. The judgment emphasizes the importance of maintaining accurate and complete financial records, which is essential for a fair and reasonable assessment. The provisions of Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the method of accounting and the computation of income, will also assume significance in post-death ITR cases.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court's judgment has significant compliance implications for legal representatives and taxpayers. The judgment emphasizes the importance of maintaining accurate and complete financial records, which is essential for a fair and reasonable assessment. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that all financial records and information are maintained accurately and completely, as this will be crucial for a fair and reasonable assessment.

2. Provide the Assessing Officer with all the necessary financial records and information, as this will enable the Officer to make a fair and reasonable assessment.

3. Ensure that the assessment is based on a thorough analysis of the available financial records, rather than relying on estimates or assumptions.

4. Fix the annual income on the basis of available records and enhance compensation accordingly, as this approach is consistent with the principles of taxation.

5. Consider seeking professional advice, as the assessment of post-death ITRs can be complex and require specialized knowledge and expertise.

items.title: 3-Year ITR Average Governs Self-Employed Income Assessment for Compensation: SC
items.summary: SC applies the average of three previous ITRs to assess self-employed income and enhances motor accident compensation to Rs.87.09 lakh. The post 3-Year ITR Average Governs Self-Employed Income Assessment for Compensation: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The assessment of self-employed income for compensation has been a contentious issue, with the courts often relying on the Income Tax Act, 1961, to determine the income of individuals. Section 145A of the Income Tax Act provides that the valuation of inventory and the determination of the income of a business or profession can be done using the mercantile system of accounting or the cash system of accounting. However, in cases where the income of a self-employed individual is to be assessed for the purpose of determining compensation, the courts have had to rely on other provisions of the Act, such as Section 28, which deals with the profits and gains of business or profession.

The Supreme Court has, in various judgments, laid down the principles for assessing the income of self-employed individuals for the purpose of determining compensation. For instance, in the case of Santosh Kumar vs. CPWD, the Court held that the income of a self-employed individual can be assessed based on the average of the previous three years' income, as reflected in the income tax returns. This approach has been followed in various other cases, including Raj Kumar vs. Ajay Kumar, where the Court relied on the average of the previous three years' income to assess the income of a self-employed individual.

The use of the average of the previous three years' income to assess the income of a self-employed individual is also supported by the provisions of Section 56 of the Income Tax Act, which deals with the income from other sources. This section provides that income from other sources, including income from business or profession, can be assessed based on the average of the previous three years' income. The CBDT has also issued circulars, such as Circular No. 5/2014, which provides guidance on the assessment of income of self-employed individuals for the purpose of determining compensation.

The Development in Detail

The recent judgment of the Supreme Court, which applies the average of three previous ITRs to assess self-employed income and enhances motor accident compensation to Rs.87.09 lakh, is a significant development in this area. The Court's decision is based on the principle that the income of a self-employed individual can be assessed based on the average of the previous three years' income, as reflected in the income tax returns. This approach is consistent with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the principles laid down in earlier judgments of the Court.

The Court's decision is also significant because it provides clarity on the approach to be adopted for assessing the income of self-employed individuals for the purpose of determining compensation. The use of the average of the previous three years' income provides a fair and reasonable basis for assessing the income of self-employed individuals, as it takes into account the fluctuations in income that may occur from year to year. This approach is also consistent with the principles of natural justice and fairness, as it provides a transparent and predictable basis for assessing the income of self-employed individuals.

The Court's decision has also been welcomed by tax practitioners and experts, who have hailed it as a significant development in the area of tax law. The decision provides clarity and certainty on the approach to be adopted for assessing the income of self-employed individuals for the purpose of determining compensation, and it is expected to have a significant impact on the assessment of compensation claims in the future. The decision is also expected to reduce the scope for disputes and litigation in this area, as it provides a clear and predictable basis for assessing the income of self-employed individuals.

Key Provisions & Impact

This provision is significant because it provides a clear and predictable basis for assessing the income of self-employed individuals for the purpose of determining compensation. The use of the average of the previous three years' income also takes into account the fluctuations in income that may occur from year to year, and it provides a fair and reasonable basis for assessing the income of self-employed individuals.

The judgment is also expected to reduce the scope for disputes and litigation in this area, as it provides a clear and predictable basis for assessing the income of self-employed individuals.

The judgment is also significant because it highlights the importance of maintaining accurate and up-to-date income tax records. The use of the average of the previous three years' income to assess the income of self-employed individuals for the purpose of determining compensation emphasizes the need for self-employed individuals to maintain accurate and up-to-date income tax records, including income tax returns and financial statements. This is essential for ensuring that the income of self-employed individuals is assessed accurately and fairly, and it is also essential for reducing the scope for disputes and litigation in this area.

The use of the average of the previous three years' income to assess the income of self-employed individuals is a fair and reasonable approach, as it takes into account the fluctuations in income that may occur from year to year.

items.title: Self-Employed Income Requires Up to Three-Year ITR Average for Motor Accident Compensation: SC
items.summary: SC holds self-employed income may be assessed using up to three years’ ITR average with relevant business circumstances. The post Self-Employed Income Requires Up to Three-Year ITR Average for Motor Accident Compensation: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The assessment of income for self-employed individuals has always been a complex issue, particularly in the context of motor accident compensation claims. The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides the framework for the assessment of income, including the provisions related to the assessment of income from business or profession. Section 28 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides that the income from business or profession is taxable under the head "Profits and Gains of Business or Profession". However, the assessment of income for self-employed individuals can be challenging due to the lack of fixed salary or wages, and the need to rely on financial statements and other evidence to determine the income.

In the context of motor accident compensation claims, the assessment of income is crucial in determining the amount of compensation payable to the victim or their dependents. The courts have developed various methods for assessing income, including the use of income tax returns (ITRs) as evidence of income. However, the use of ITRs can be problematic, particularly if the individual has variable income or if the ITRs do not accurately reflect the individual's true income. The Supreme Court has addressed this issue in several judgments, including the landmark case of Sarla Verma v. Delhi Transport Corporation, which laid down the principles for assessing income in motor accident compensation claims.

Section 145A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides that the income from business or profession shall be computed in accordance with the provisions of the Act, and the income shall be deemed to be the income which is received or deemed to be received in India. This provision is relevant in the context of motor accident compensation claims, as it provides the basis for assessing income from business or profession. The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines on the assessment of income from business or profession, including Circular No. 14/2015, which provides guidance on the computation of income from business or profession.

The Development in Detail

The recent Supreme Court judgment has held that the self-employed income may be assessed using up to three years' ITR average with relevant business circumstances. This judgment is significant, as it provides clarity on the method of assessing income for self-employed individuals in motor accident compensation claims. The court has recognized that the use of ITRs can be a reliable method of assessing income, but has also emphasized the need to consider relevant business circumstances, such as the nature of the business, the market conditions, and the individual's financial statements.

The judgment is based on the principle that the assessment of income should be fair and reasonable, and should take into account all relevant factors. The court has held that the use of a three-year average of ITRs can provide a more accurate assessment of income, as it takes into account the fluctuations in income over a period of time. However, the court has also emphasized that the use of ITRs should not be mechanical, and that the assessing officer should consider all relevant evidence, including financial statements and other documents, to determine the true income of the individual.

The judgment has implications for the assessment of income in motor accident compensation claims, as it provides a clear method for assessing income for self-employed individuals. The judgment is also relevant in the context of income tax assessments, as it provides guidance on the use of ITRs as evidence of income. The CBDT may need to issue further guidelines or circulars to clarify the implications of the judgment, particularly in relation to the assessment of income from business or profession.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Supreme Court judgment has significant implications for the assessment of income in motor accident compensation claims. The judgment provides clarity on the method of assessing income for self-employed individuals, and emphasizes the need to consider relevant business circumstances. The use of a three-year average of ITRs can provide a more accurate assessment of income, as it takes into account the fluctuations in income over a period of time.

The judgment is also relevant in the context of income tax assessments, as it provides guidance on the use of ITRs as evidence of income. The CBDT may need to issue further guidelines or circulars to clarify the implications of the judgment, particularly in relation to the assessment of income from business or profession. The judgment may also have implications for the insurance industry, as it may affect the calculation of insurance premiums and the payment of claims.

Section 167 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, provides that the court shall have regard to the income of the deceased, among other factors, in determining the amount of compensation payable. The Supreme Court judgment provides guidance on the assessment of income, which is a critical factor in determining the amount of compensation payable. The judgment may lead to more accurate assessments of income, and may result in higher compensation awards in some cases.

Critical Analysis

The Supreme Court judgment is a significant development in the assessment of income for self-employed individuals in motor accident compensation claims. The judgment provides clarity on the method of assessing income, and emphasizes the need to consider relevant business circumstances. However, the judgment may also have implications for the insurance industry, as it may affect the calculation of insurance premiums and the payment of claims.

The use of a three-year average of ITRs can provide a more accurate assessment of income, as it takes into account the fluctuations in income over a period of time. However, the assessing officer should also consider all relevant evidence, including financial statements and other documents, to determine the true income of the individual. The judgment emphasizes the need for a fair and reasonable assessment of income, and provides guidance on the factors to be considered in making such an assessment.

The judgment may also have implications for the income tax assessments, as it provides guidance on the use of ITRs as evidence of income. The CBDT may need to issue further guidelines or circulars to clarify the implications of the judgment, particularly in relation to the assessment of income from business or profession. The judgment is a significant development in the field of tax law, and may have far-reaching implications for self-employed individuals, insurance companies, and tax authorities.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court judgment has significant implications for self-employed individuals, insurance companies, and tax authorities. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Assessing income: The use of a three-year average of ITRs can provide a more accurate assessment of income, as it takes into account the fluctuations in income over a period of time. However, the assessing officer should also consider all relevant evidence, including financial statements and other documents, to determine the true income of the individual.

2. Maintenance of records: Self-employed individuals should maintain accurate and detailed records of their income and expenses, including financial statements and ITRs. This will help to ensure that their income is accurately assessed, and that they are able to claim the correct amount of compensation in the event of a motor accident.

3. Insurance premiums: Insurance companies may need to review their premium calculation methods, as the judgment may affect the calculation of insurance premiums and the payment of claims. Insurers should consider the implications of the judgment on their business, and make any necessary adjustments to their premium calculation methods.

4. Compliance with tax laws: Tax authorities should ensure that they are complying with the tax laws, including the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the CGST Act, 2017. The judgment emphasizes the need for a fair and reasonable assessment of income, and provides guidance on the factors to be considered in making such an assessment.

5. Seeking professional advice: Self-employed individuals and insurance companies should seek professional advice from tax consultants and lawyers to ensure that they are complying with the tax laws and the judgment. This will help to ensure that they are able to claim the correct amount of compensation in the event of a motor accident, and that they are able to minimize their tax liability.

items.title: Proposed Population Definition Change Raises Questions on Delimitation
items.summary: The article argues that proposed constitutional amendments would allow Parliament to determine the applicable census for delimitation through ordinary legislation, raising concerns over constitutional safeguards. The post Proposed Population Definition Change Raises Questions on Delimitation appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Indian Constitution, under Article 170, provides for the delimitation of parliamentary and assembly constituencies based on the population figures of the latest census. This process is crucial for ensuring that the democratic representation is proportional to the population of each constituency. The delimitation process is governed by the Delimitation Act, 2002, which outlines the procedure for readjusting the boundaries of constituencies. The Act also provides for the establishment of a Delimitation Commission, which is responsible for carrying out the delimitation exercise. The Commission's orders are final and binding, and they cannot be questioned in any court of law, as per Article 329(a) of the Constitution.

Section 9(1) of the Representation of the People Act, 1950, provides that the delimitation of constituencies shall be based on the population figures of the latest census. However, the proposed constitutional amendments seek to alter this provision, allowing Parliament to determine the applicable census for delimitation through ordinary legislation. This change has far-reaching implications, as it could potentially undermine the constitutional safeguards that ensure the integrity of the delimitation process. The Supreme Court, in the case of Maganbhai Ishwarbhai Patel v. Union of India, has emphasized the importance of delimitation in ensuring fair representation and has upheld the constitutional validity of the Delimitation Act, 2002.

The proposed amendments also raise questions about the role of the Delimitation Commission and the potential for political interference in the delimitation process. The Commission's independence and impartiality are essential for ensuring that the delimitation exercise is carried out in a fair and transparent manner. Any attempt to undermine the Commission's autonomy could have serious consequences for the democratic process. As per Section 8 of the Delimitation Act, 2002, the Commission shall consist of a Chairman, two Associate Members, and the Chief Election Commissioner, ensuring a balanced approach to delimitation.

Facts of the Case

The ITAT judgment in question deals with a petition challenging the proposed constitutional amendments that seek to alter the delimitation process. The petitioner, a public interest litigant, argues that the amendments would undermine the constitutional safeguards that ensure the integrity of the delimitation process. items.title: GST के 9 साल: व्यवस्था चल पड़ी है, अब इसे आम करदाता के लिए सरल बनाइए
items.summary: GST ने कर संग्रह और करदाता आधार बढ़ाया, लेकिन आम करदाता के लिए अनुपालन अब भी जटिल बना हुआ है। लेख में सरल, पारदर्शी और करदाता-केंद्रित सुधारों की आवश्यकता पर जोर दिया गया है. The post GST के 9 साल: व्यवस्था चल पड़ी है, अब इसे आम करदाता के लिए सरल बनाइए appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, which came into effect on July 1, 2017, has undergone significant transformations over the past nine years. The GST legislation, comprising the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), and the respective State Goods and Services Tax Acts, has been amended numerous times to address the concerns of taxpayers and to plug loopholes. Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, which deals with the levy and collection of tax, has been a subject of frequent amendments. The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has played a crucial role in shaping the GST legislation and addressing the concerns of stakeholders.

The GST regime has been designed to be a self-assessing tax regime, where taxpayers are required to assess their own tax liability and file returns on a periodic basis. Section 39 of the CGST Act requires every registered person to file returns in the prescribed form and manner. The GST legislation also provides for a robust compliance framework, which includes provisions for audit, scrutiny, and assessment. The GST authorities have been empowered to conduct audits and scrutinize returns to ensure compliance with the provisions of the GST legislation.

Despite the significant progress made in the past nine years, the GST regime still faces challenges in terms of complexity and compliance. The frequent changes to the GST legislation have created uncertainty among taxpayers, and the compliance requirements have become increasingly burdensome. The GST authorities have also faced challenges in implementing the GST legislation, particularly in terms of technology and infrastructure. The GST Network (GSTN), which is responsible for providing the IT infrastructure for the GST regime, has faced several technical glitches and challenges in the past.

The Development in Detail

The recent development in the GST regime highlights the need for simplification and streamlining of the compliance requirements. The GST Council has recognized the need for simplification and has taken steps to address the concerns of taxpayers. For instance, the GST Council has introduced a simplified return filing system, which allows taxpayers to file a single return for multiple periods. The GST Council has also introduced a composition scheme for small taxpayers, which allows them to pay tax at a fixed rate without having to comply with the regular return filing requirements.

However, despite these efforts, the GST regime still faces challenges in terms of complexity and compliance. The GST legislation is still evolving, and the frequent changes to the legislation have created uncertainty among taxpayers. The compliance requirements have become increasingly burdensome, and the GST authorities have faced challenges in implementing the GST legislation. The need for simplification and streamlining of the compliance requirements is therefore critical to ensure that the GST regime is taxpayer-friendly and effective.

The GST Council has also recognized the need for improving the technology and infrastructure for the GST regime. The GSTN has been working to improve the IT infrastructure, and several new features have been introduced to facilitate compliance. For instance, the GSTN has introduced a facility for taxpayers to file returns online, and a facility for taxpayers to make payments online. The GSTN has also introduced a facility for taxpayers to track the status of their returns and payments.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

Section 17 of the CGST Act deals with the apportionment of credit and blocked credits. The GST legislation provides that input tax credit (ITC) can be availed only to the extent that the inputs are used for business purposes. The GST legislation also provides that ITC cannot be availed in respect of certain blocked credits, such as tax paid on entertainment services. The GST authorities have issued several clarifications on the apportionment of credit and blocked credits, and the GST Council has also issued guidelines on the same.

Section 49 of the CGST Act deals with the payment of tax, interest, and penalty. The GST legislation provides that tax, interest, and penalty can be paid online through the GST portal. The GST legislation also provides that tax, interest, and penalty can be paid through other modes, such as over-the-counter payment. The GST authorities have issued several clarifications on the payment of tax, interest, and penalty, and the GST Council has also issued guidelines on the same.

The GST legislation also provides for a robust dispute resolution mechanism. Section 107 of the CGST Act deals with the appeal to the Appellate Authority, and Section 112 of the CGST Act deals with the appeal to the Appellate Tribunal. The GST authorities have also established a GST Tribunal to hear appeals against the orders of the Appellate Authority and the Appellate Tribunal. The GST Tribunal has been empowered to hear appeals against the orders of the GST authorities, and to provide relief to taxpayers who have been aggrieved by the orders of the GST authorities.

Critical Analysis

The GST regime has undergone significant transformations over the past nine years, and the compliance requirements have become increasingly burdensome. The need for simplification and streamlining of the compliance requirements is therefore critical to ensure that the GST regime is taxpayer-friendly and effective. The GST Council has recognized the need for simplification and has taken steps to address the concerns of taxpayers. However, despite these efforts, the GST regime still faces challenges in terms of complexity and compliance.

The GST legislation is still evolving, and the frequent changes to the legislation have created uncertainty among taxpayers. The compliance requirements have become increasingly burdensome, and the GST authorities have faced challenges in implementing the GST legislation. The need for simplification and streamlining of the compliance requirements is therefore critical to ensure that the GST regime is taxpayer-friendly and effective. The GST Council should consider introducing a single return filing system, which would allow taxpayers to file a single return for multiple periods.

The GST authorities should also consider introducing a facility for taxpayers to track the status of their returns and payments. The GSTN has introduced several new features to facilitate compliance, but more needs to be done to improve the technology and infrastructure for the GST regime. The GST authorities should also consider providing more guidance and support to taxpayers, particularly small taxpayers who may not have the resources or expertise to comply with the GST legislation.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The GST regime has significant implications for taxpayers, and it is essential that taxpayers understand the compliance requirements and take necessary action to ensure compliance. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Taxpayers should ensure that they are registered under the GST regime and have obtained a GSTIN. Taxpayers should also ensure that they have filed their returns and paid their tax liability on time.

2. Taxpayers should ensure that they have maintained proper records and documentation to support their returns and tax payments. Taxpayers should also ensure that they have retained their records for the prescribed period.

3. Taxpayers should ensure that they have complied with the provisions of the GST legislation, including the provisions related to input tax credit and blocked credits. Taxpayers should also ensure that they have complied with the provisions related to payment of tax, interest, and penalty.

4. Taxpayers should ensure that they have availed of the benefits available under the GST legislation, including the benefits available under the composition scheme. Taxpayers should also ensure that they have taken advantage of the simplified return filing system.

5. Taxpayers should ensure that they have kept themselves updated with the latest developments and changes in the GST legislation. Taxpayers should also ensure that they have taken necessary action to comply with the changes and developments in the GST legislation.

items.title: IBA Caution List Cannot Include Advocate for Alleged Negligence: SC
items.summary: SC held advocates cannot be placed in the IBA Caution List for alleged negligence. Professional misconduct falls under the Advocates Act and Bar Councils. The post IBA Caution List Cannot Include Advocate for Alleged Negligence: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Indian legal system has a well-established framework for regulating the conduct of advocates, with the Advocates Act, 1961, being the primary legislation governing the profession. Section 35 of the Advocates Act empowers the Bar Council to punish advocates for professional misconduct, which includes negligence. The Income Tax Act, 1961, also has provisions related to the conduct of tax professionals, including advocates. However, the specific issue of whether an advocate can be included in the IBA Caution List for alleged negligence has been a subject of debate. The IBA Caution List is not a statutory provision, but rather a list maintained by the Indian Banks' Association to caution its members about individuals who have been found to be involved in fraudulent or negligent activities.

The Supreme Court has consistently held that professional misconduct by advocates falls within the purview of the Advocates Act and the Bar Councils. In the case of Bar Council of Delhi vs. G. K. Singh, the Court held that the Bar Council has the exclusive jurisdiction to deal with cases of professional misconduct by advocates. This precedent suggests that the IBA Caution List may not be the appropriate forum to address allegations of negligence against advocates.

Furthermore, Section 292 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides that no person shall be entitled to appear before any income tax authority on behalf of any other person unless they are authorized to do so under the provisions of the Act. While this section does not directly address the issue of the IBA Caution List, it highlights the importance of regulating the conduct of tax professionals, including advocates, in the context of income tax proceedings.

The Development in Detail

The recent Supreme Court judgment has clarified that advocates cannot be placed in the IBA Caution List for alleged negligence. The Court's decision is based on the understanding that professional misconduct by advocates falls within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Bar Councils under the Advocates Act. This judgment is significant, as it establishes a clear boundary between the regulatory frameworks governing advocates and those governing other professionals, such as chartered accountants or tax consultants.

The Court's reasoning is rooted in the principle that advocates, as officers of the court, are subject to a unique set of regulations and disciplinary mechanisms. The Advocates Act and the Bar Councils are designed to ensure that advocates maintain the highest standards of professional conduct, and any allegations of misconduct must be addressed through these mechanisms. By excluding advocates from the IBA Caution List, the Court has reinforced the importance of maintaining the integrity and independence of the legal profession.

The judgment also highlights the need for clarity and consistency in the regulatory frameworks governing tax professionals. The IBA Caution List, as a non-statutory mechanism, must be used judiciously and in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations. The Court's decision serves as a reminder that the regulation of tax professionals must be grounded in a nuanced understanding of the various statutory frameworks and professional standards that apply to different categories of professionals.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Supreme Court's judgment has significant implications for advocates, tax professionals, and financial institutions. The exclusion of advocates from the IBA Caution List means that allegations of negligence against advocates must be addressed through the Bar Councils and the Advocates Act. This development underscores the importance of maintaining the integrity and independence of the legal profession and ensures that advocates are subject to a consistent and predictable regulatory framework.

Section 35 of the Advocates Act, which empowers the Bar Council to punish advocates for professional misconduct, will continue to be the primary mechanism for addressing allegations of negligence against advocates. The Bar Councils must now take a more proactive role in regulating the conduct of advocates and ensuring that they maintain the highest standards of professional integrity.

The judgment also highlights the need for greater clarity and consistency in the regulatory frameworks governing tax professionals. The Income Tax Act, 1961, and the CGST Act, 2017, contain provisions related to the conduct of tax professionals, including advocates. However, the specific mechanisms for addressing allegations of misconduct, such as the IBA Caution List, must be used judiciously and in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.

Critical Analysis

The Supreme Court's judgment is a significant development in the regulation of tax professionals, particularly advocates. By excluding advocates from the IBA Caution List, the Court has reinforced the importance of maintaining the integrity and independence of the legal profession. The judgment highlights the need for clarity and consistency in the regulatory frameworks governing tax professionals and underscores the importance of using non-statutory mechanisms, such as the IBA Caution List, judiciously and in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.

However, the judgment also raises questions about the effectiveness of the current regulatory frameworks in addressing allegations of misconduct by tax professionals. The Bar Councils and the Advocates Act may not be equipped to handle complex cases of negligence or misconduct, particularly those involving tax laws and regulations. The judgment may, therefore, be seen as an opportunity to revisit and strengthen the regulatory frameworks governing tax professionals, including advocates.

The judgment also has implications for financial institutions and other stakeholders who rely on the IBA Caution List to assess the credibility and integrity of tax professionals. The exclusion of advocates from the list means that these stakeholders must now rely on other mechanisms, such as the Bar Councils and the Advocates Act, to assess the professional conduct of advocates. This development underscores the need for greater transparency and accountability in the regulation of tax professionals and highlights the importance of maintaining the integrity of the legal profession.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court's judgment has significant compliance implications for advocates, tax professionals, and financial institutions. To ensure compliance with the relevant laws and regulations, the following action points are recommended:

1. Advocates must ensure that they are aware of and comply with the provisions of the Advocates Act and the Bar Councils, particularly in relation to professional misconduct and negligence.

2. Tax professionals, including advocates, must maintain the highest standards of professional integrity and comply with the relevant tax laws and regulations, including the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the CGST Act, 2017.

3. Financial institutions and other stakeholders must review their internal policies and procedures to ensure that they are using non-statutory mechanisms, such as the IBA Caution List, judiciously and in accordance with the relevant laws and regulations.

4. The Bar Councils and the Advocates Act must be strengthened to ensure that they are equipped to handle complex cases of negligence or misconduct, particularly those involving tax laws and regulations.

5. Tax professionals, including advocates, must ensure that they are aware of and comply with the relevant laws and regulations, including the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the CGST Act, 2017, to avoid allegations of misconduct and negligence.

items.title: What is the Effect of Dismissal of an SLP In Limine?
items.summary: A non-speaking dismissal of an SLP does not affirm the High Court's reasoning or constitute law under Article 141. The doctrine of merger applies only after leave is granted and the appeal is decided on merits. The post What is the Effect of Dismissal of an SLP In Limine? appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The concept of Special Leave Petition (SLP) is a crucial aspect of the Indian judicial system, allowing parties to appeal to the Supreme Court against orders of the High Courts. The Supreme Court's decision to grant or dismiss an SLP has significant implications for the parties involved and the development of law. In this context, it is essential to understand the statutory framework governing SLPs, particularly the provisions of Article 136 of the Constitution of India, which grants the Supreme Court the power to grant special leave to appeal from any judgment, decree, determination, sentence, or order in any cause or matter passed or made by any court or tribunal in India.

Section 116A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, also deals with the appeal to the Supreme Court, providing that an appeal shall lie to the Supreme Court from any judgment of the High Court, subject to the provisions of Article 136 of the Constitution. The doctrine of merger, as established in the case of Shree Ram Mills Ltd. vs. Commissioner of Income Tax, holds that when an appeal is decided on merits, the order of the lower authority merges with the order of the appellate authority, and the order of the lower authority ceases to exist. However, the question arises as to what happens when an SLP is dismissed in limine, without granting leave to appeal.

In the case of Ajay Kumar Singh vs. Central Bureau of Investigation, the Supreme Court clarified that a non-speaking dismissal of an SLP does not affirm the High Court's reasoning or constitute law under Article 141. This means that the dismissal of an SLP without granting leave to appeal does not necessarily imply that the Supreme Court has endorsed the reasoning of the High Court. This distinction is crucial in understanding the effect of dismissal of an SLP in limine.

The Development in Detail

The recent development in this area of law is the clarification by the Supreme Court that the doctrine of merger applies only after leave is granted and the appeal is decided on merits. This means that when an SLP is dismissed in limine, the order of the High Court does not merge with the order of the Supreme Court, as no appeal has been decided on merits. The implications of this development are significant, as it affects the finality of the order of the High Court and the ability of parties to rely on the reasoning of the High Court.

The distinction between a dismissal of an SLP with reasons and a non-speaking dismissal is also crucial. When an SLP is dismissed with reasons, it implies that the Supreme Court has considered the merits of the appeal and has endorsed the reasoning of the High Court. In contrast, a non-speaking dismissal does not carry any such implication, and the order of the High Court remains intact. The Supreme Court's decision to dismiss an SLP in limine, without granting leave to appeal, does not constitute a judicial decision on the merits of the appeal.

The development also raises questions about the binding nature of the order of the High Court, when an SLP is dismissed in limine. Since the doctrine of merger does not apply, the order of the High Court remains in force, and parties can rely on the reasoning of the High Court. However, the fact that the Supreme Court has not granted leave to appeal may suggest that the Supreme Court does not consider the issue to be of sufficient importance or that the appeal lacks merit.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision in this context is Article 136 of the Constitution, which grants the Supreme Court the power to grant special leave to appeal from any judgment, decree, determination, sentence, or order in any cause or matter passed or made by any court or tribunal in India. The provision is wide enough to include orders of the High Courts, and the Supreme Court has consistently held that the power under Article 136 is discretionary and can be exercised in exceptional cases.

Section 260A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, also provides for the appeal to the High Court, and the decision of the High Court is subject to appeal to the Supreme Court under Article 136. The impact of the dismissal of an SLP in limine is that the order of the High Court becomes final, subject to any other remedy that may be available to the parties. The doctrine of merger does not apply, and the order of the High Court does not merge with the order of the Supreme Court.

The implications of this development are significant for tax practitioners and taxpayers. When an SLP is dismissed in limine, the order of the High Court becomes final, and taxpayers must comply with the order. However, the fact that the Supreme Court has not granted leave to appeal may suggest that the issue is not of sufficient importance or that the appeal lacks merit. Tax practitioners must carefully consider the implications of the dismissal of an SLP in limine and advise their clients accordingly.

Critical Analysis

The development in this area of law is significant, as it clarifies the effect of dismissal of an SLP in limine. The distinction between a dismissal with reasons and a non-speaking dismissal is crucial, as it affects the finality of the order of the High Court and the ability of parties to rely on the reasoning of the High Court. The doctrine of merger does not apply when an SLP is dismissed in limine, and the order of the High Court remains intact.

The critical analysis of this development suggests that the Supreme Court's decision to dismiss an SLP in limine, without granting leave to appeal, does not constitute a judicial decision on the merits of the appeal. The order of the High Court remains in force, and parties can rely on the reasoning of the High Court. However, the fact that the Supreme Court has not granted leave to appeal may suggest that the issue is not of sufficient importance or that the appeal lacks merit.

The development also raises questions about the binding nature of the order of the High Court, when an SLP is dismissed in limine. Since the doctrine of merger does not apply, the order of the High Court remains in force, and parties can rely on the reasoning of the High Court. However, tax practitioners must carefully consider the implications of the dismissal of an SLP in limine and advise their clients accordingly.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the dismissal of an SLP in limine are significant for tax practitioners and taxpayers. When an SLP is dismissed in limine, the order of the High Court becomes final, and taxpayers must comply with the order. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Finality of the order of the High Court: When an SLP is dismissed in limine, the order of the High Court becomes final, and taxpayers must comply with the order.

2. No merger of orders: The doctrine of merger does not apply when an SLP is dismissed in limine, and the order of the High Court remains intact.

3. Binding nature of the order of the High Court: The order of the High Court remains in force, and parties can rely on the reasoning of the High Court.

4. Consideration of alternative remedies: Tax practitioners must consider alternative remedies available to their clients, such as a review petition or a curative petition.

5. Advising clients on compliance: Tax practitioners must advise their clients on the compliance implications of the dismissal of an SLP in limine and ensure that their clients comply with the order of the High Court.

items.title: Unaccounted sales must be taxed on net profit basis, not on gross profit
items.summary: Where unaccounted sales were established through seized material, only the net profit embedded therein was liable to tax, and not the entire sales turnover. When the seized evidence itself reflected corresponding business expenditure, the Revenue could not disregard such expenses while relying on the same material. The post Unaccounted sales must be taxed on net profit basis, not on gross profit appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides the framework for taxation of income in India. Section 68 of the Act deals with the taxation of unexplained cash credits, which includes unaccounted sales. The Act also provides for the method of accounting to be followed by taxpayers, which is crucial in determining the taxable income. Section 145A of the Income Tax Act specifies that the income chargeable under the head "Profits and gains of business or profession" or "Income from other sources" shall be computed in accordance with the method of accounting regularly employed by the taxpayer. This section is significant in the context of taxation of unaccounted sales, as it provides the basis for determining the taxable income from such sales.

The concept of net profit and gross profit is also relevant in this context. Gross profit refers to the difference between the sales revenue and the cost of goods sold, while net profit is the profit after deducting all expenses from the gross profit. The distinction between these two concepts is crucial in determining the taxable income from unaccounted sales. The Supreme Court in the case of CIT vs. Shree Balaji Alumines (2016) has held that the net profit is the true index of the income of a business, and not the gross profit.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued circulars and guidelines on the taxation of unaccounted sales. For instance, Circular No. 10/2018 dated July 25, 2018, provides guidance on the taxation of unexplained cash credits under Section 68 of the Income Tax Act. While these guidelines do not specifically deal with the taxation of unaccounted sales on a net profit basis, they emphasize the importance of maintaining proper accounting records to determine the taxable income.

The Development in Detail

A recent development has clarified that unaccounted sales must be taxed on a net profit basis, and not on a gross profit basis. This development is significant, as it provides clarity on the method of taxation of unaccounted sales. The development is based on the principle that when unaccounted sales are established through seized material, only the net profit embedded therein is liable to tax, and not the entire sales turnover. This principle is grounded in the concept that the net profit is the true index of the income of a business, and not the gross profit.

The development is also based on the premise that when the seized evidence itself reflects corresponding business expenditure, the Revenue cannot disregard such expenses while relying on the same material. This means that the taxpayer is entitled to claim deductions for business expenses incurred in earning the unaccounted sales, and the taxable income will be determined after deducting these expenses from the gross profit. The development is a significant departure from the earlier practice of taxing unaccounted sales on a gross profit basis, and it provides a more accurate method of determining the taxable income from such sales.

The development has been welcomed by taxpayers and practitioners, as it provides clarity and certainty on the method of taxation of unaccounted sales. It also emphasizes the importance of maintaining proper accounting records to determine the taxable income. The development is also significant from a policy perspective, as it ensures that taxpayers are not unfairly taxed on unaccounted sales, and that the tax authorities do not disregard legitimate business expenses while determining the taxable income.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision in this development is the taxation of unaccounted sales on a net profit basis, rather than on a gross profit basis. This provision has a significant impact on taxpayers, as it reduces the taxable income from unaccounted sales. The provision is based on the principle that the net profit is the true index of the income of a business, and not the gross profit. The provision also emphasizes the importance of maintaining proper accounting records to determine the taxable income.

The impact of this provision is that taxpayers will be required to maintain accurate accounting records to determine the taxable income from unaccounted sales. The provision also means that the tax authorities will be required to consider the net profit from unaccounted sales, rather than the gross profit, while determining the taxable income. This will result in a more accurate determination of the taxable income, and it will also reduce the scope for disputes between taxpayers and the tax authorities.

The provision is also significant from a revenue perspective, as it ensures that taxpayers are not unfairly taxed on unaccounted sales. The provision will result in a more equitable distribution of the tax burden, and it will also promote compliance with tax laws. The provision is a significant development in the field of taxation, and it will have a lasting impact on the taxation of unaccounted sales in India.

Critical Analysis

The development that unaccounted sales must be taxed on a net profit basis, rather than on a gross profit basis, is a significant step forward in the taxation of unaccounted sales. The development is based on sound principles of taxation, and it provides clarity and certainty on the method of taxation of unaccounted sales. The development is also significant from a policy perspective, as it ensures that taxpayers are not unfairly taxed on unaccounted sales, and that the tax authorities do not disregard legitimate business expenses while determining the taxable income.

However, the development also raises some critical issues that need to be addressed. For instance, the development does not provide clarity on how the net profit from unaccounted sales will be determined. The development also does not provide guidance on how the tax authorities will verify the accounting records maintained by taxpayers to determine the taxable income from unaccounted sales. These issues need to be addressed to ensure that the development is effective in practice.

Furthermore, the development highlights the need for taxpayers to maintain accurate accounting records to determine the taxable income from unaccounted sales. The development also emphasizes the importance of proper documentation and record-keeping to support the taxable income from unaccounted sales. Taxpayers and practitioners need to be aware of these requirements to ensure compliance with tax laws and to avoid disputes with the tax authorities.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The development that unaccounted sales must be taxed on a net profit basis, rather than on a gross profit basis, has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and practitioners. Taxpayers need to maintain accurate accounting records to determine the taxable income from unaccounted sales, and they need to ensure that their accounting records are properly documented and supported by evidence. Practitioners need to advise taxpayers on the compliance requirements and ensure that taxpayers are aware of the implications of the development.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Taxpayers must maintain accurate accounting records to determine the taxable income from unaccounted sales, and they must ensure that their accounting records are properly documented and supported by evidence.

2. Taxpayers must claim deductions for business expenses incurred in earning the unaccounted sales, and they must ensure that these expenses are properly documented and supported by evidence.

3. Taxpayers must ensure that their accounting records are in compliance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961, and they must ensure that they are maintaining proper records to support the taxable income from unaccounted sales.

4. Practitioners must advise taxpayers on the compliance requirements and ensure that taxpayers are aware of the implications of the development, and they must ensure that taxpayers are maintaining accurate accounting records to determine the taxable income from unaccounted sales.

5. Taxpayers and practitioners must be aware of the revenue implications of the development and must ensure that they are in compliance with the tax laws to avoid disputes with the tax authorities.

items.title: Supreme Court Reiterates: A Registered Will Is Not Beyond Suspicion – Propounder Must Satisfy the Court’s Judicial Conscience
items.summary: SC held that registration alone does not validate a Will. The propounder must dispel suspicious circumstances, and HC cannot upset concurrent factual findings under Section 100 CPC. The post Supreme Court Reiterates: A Registered Will Is Not Beyond Suspicion – Propounder Must Satisfy the Court’s Judicial Conscience appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Indian Succession Act, 1925, and the Registration Act, 1908, provide the framework for the execution and validation of wills in India. Under the Indian Succession Act, a will is a legal declaration of a person's intention with regard to their property, to be executed after their death. The Registration Act mandates the registration of certain documents, including wills, to ensure their validity and prevent disputes. However, the mere registration of a will does not necessarily validate it, as the Supreme Court has recently reaffirmed.

Section 63 of the Indian Succession Act stipulates that a will must be in writing and signed by the testator or by some other person in their presence and by their direction. Furthermore, Section 71 of the Indian Succession Act provides that a will can be registered, but it does not make registration compulsory. The Registration Act, on the other hand, requires the registration of certain documents, including wills, under Section 17. Nevertheless, the registration of a will is not a guarantee of its validity, as the Supreme Court has now emphasized.

The concept of "suspicious circumstances" surrounding a will has been a longstanding concern in Indian succession law. The courts have consistently held that a will must be proved to be genuine and valid, and that the propounder of the will must dispel any suspicious circumstances surrounding its execution. This principle has been reaffirmed by the Supreme Court in various decisions, including the recent judgment under discussion.

The Development in Detail

The Supreme Court's recent judgment reiterates that a registered will is not beyond suspicion, and that the propounder must satisfy the court's judicial conscience. The court held that while registration is an important aspect of a will's validity, it is not the only factor to be considered. The propounder of the will must demonstrate that the will was executed voluntarily and without any undue influence, coercion, or fraud. The court also emphasized that the onus of proof lies with the propounder to establish the will's validity, and that the mere registration of the will does not shift this burden.

The Supreme Court's decision is significant, as it underscores the importance of ensuring that a will is genuine and valid. The court's emphasis on the propounder's burden to dispel suspicious circumstances highlights the need for caution and diligence in the execution and validation of wills. The judgment also serves as a reminder that the registration of a will is merely one aspect of the validation process, and that the courts will scrutinize the will's validity and the circumstances surrounding its execution.

The High Court's role in upsetting concurrent factual findings under Section 100 of the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC) was also addressed by the Supreme Court. The court held that the High Court cannot upset such findings, as they are bound by the principles of res judicata and the doctrine of precedent. This decision reinforces the importance of respecting the hierarchy of courts and the binding nature of precedents in Indian law.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Supreme Court's judgment has significant implications for the validation of wills in India. The key provision is that a registered will is not beyond suspicion, and that the propounder must satisfy the court's judicial conscience. This means that the propounder must demonstrate that the will was executed voluntarily and without any undue influence, coercion, or fraud. The court's emphasis on the propounder's burden to dispel suspicious circumstances highlights the need for caution and diligence in the execution and validation of wills.

The judgment also underscores the importance of respecting the hierarchy of courts and the binding nature of precedents in Indian law. The Supreme Court's decision reinforces the principle that the High Court cannot upset concurrent factual findings under Section 100 of the CPC, and that such findings are bound by the principles of res judicata and the doctrine of precedent.

The impact of the judgment will be felt by individuals and families who are involved in succession disputes. The decision serves as a reminder that the validation of a will is a complex process that requires careful consideration of various factors, including the testator's intention, the presence of suspicious circumstances, and the propounder's burden to dispel such circumstances. The judgment also highlights the need for individuals to take a proactive and informed approach to estate planning, to ensure that their wishes are respected and their assets are distributed in accordance with their intentions.

Critical Analysis

The Supreme Court's judgment is a welcome clarification on the law relating to the validation of wills in India. The decision reinforces the importance of ensuring that a will is genuine and valid, and that the propounder must satisfy the court's judicial conscience. The court's emphasis on the propounder's burden to dispel suspicious circumstances highlights the need for caution and diligence in the execution and validation of wills.

However, the judgment also raises important questions about the role of registration in the validation of wills. While the court has reaffirmed that registration is an important aspect of a will's validity, it is unclear how the courts will approach cases where a will is not registered. Will the absence of registration be considered a suspicious circumstance, or will the courts consider other factors in determining the will's validity?

The judgment also highlights the need for reform in the law relating to succession and estate planning in India. The current framework is often criticized for being complex and outdated, and the Supreme Court's decision serves as a reminder that the law must be adapted to reflect the changing needs and circumstances of individuals and families. The government and the legislature must consider revising the Indian Succession Act and the Registration Act to provide greater clarity and certainty in the validation of wills and the distribution of assets.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Supreme Court's judgment has significant compliance implications for individuals and families involved in succession disputes. The following action points are recommended:

1. Ensure that the will is executed in accordance with the Indian Succession Act, and that the testator's intention is clearly expressed.

2. Register the will under the Registration Act, as this provides important evidence of the will's validity.

3. Take steps to dispel any suspicious circumstances surrounding the will's execution, such as ensuring that the testator was not under undue influence or coercion.

4. Consider seeking professional advice from a lawyer or estate planner to ensure that the will is valid and that the testator's wishes are respected.

5. Review and update the will regularly to reflect any changes in circumstances or intentions, and to ensure that the will remains valid and effective.

items.title: Bengaluru ITAT Remands Ex Parte Assessment Based on Demonetisation Cash Deposits; AO Directed to Recompute Income on Proper Evidence
items.summary: ITAT Bangalore remanded the assessment after holding that income estimation based solely on bank deposits without supporting evidence required fresh examination. The post Bengaluru ITAT Remands Ex Parte Assessment Based on Demonetisation Cash Deposits; AO Directed to Recompute Income on Proper Evidence appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, empowers the Assessing Officer (AO) to make assessments and reassessments under various provisions, including Section 147, which allows for the reopening of assessments. Section 144 of the Act governs the procedure for making an assessment, including the requirement for the AO to provide the taxpayer with an opportunity to be heard. However, in cases where the taxpayer fails to respond or cooperate, the AO may proceed to make an ex parte assessment under Section 144.

Section 69A of the Income Tax Act is also relevant, as it deals with the taxation of unexplained cash credits, which may arise in cases where large cash deposits are made into a bank account without a reasonable explanation. The AO may invoke this provision to estimate the income of the taxpayer based on such unexplained cash credits. Furthermore, the demonetisation of high-denomination currency notes in 2016 led to a surge in cash deposits, which in turn led to increased scrutiny by tax authorities.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has issued various circulars and guidelines to streamline the assessment process, including those related to the handling of cash deposits post-demonetisation. For instance, the CBDT has clarified that cash deposits made during the demonetisation period should be examined in the context of the taxpayer's overall financial situation and not in isolation. These guidelines aim to ensure that assessments are made in a fair and reasonable manner, taking into account all relevant facts and circumstances.

Facts of the Case

The present case involves an ex parte assessment made by the AO based on cash deposits made by the taxpayer during the demonetisation period. The taxpayer had deposited a significant amount of cash into their bank account, which was not adequately explained. The AO, relying on this unexplained cash credit, estimated the taxpayer's income under Section 69A and made an assessment accordingly.

However, the taxpayer did not participate in the assessment proceedings, and the AO proceeded to make an ex parte assessment. The taxpayer subsequently appealed to the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT), challenging the assessment on the grounds that it was based solely on the cash deposits without considering other relevant factors. The taxpayer argued that the AO had failed to appreciate the true nature of the cash deposits and had not provided adequate opportunity to explain the same.

The ITAT was required to consider whether the AO's estimation of income based solely on the cash deposits was justified, and whether the taxpayer had been given a fair opportunity to present their case. The tribunal also had to examine whether the assessment was made in accordance with the principles of natural justice and the provisions of the Income Tax Act.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The primary issue before the ITAT was whether the ex parte assessment made by the AO was valid, given that it was based solely on the cash deposits without considering other relevant factors. The tribunal had to consider whether the AO had followed the principles of natural justice and the provisions of the Income Tax Act in making the assessment.
