items.title: Anticipatory Bail to Former IAS Officer was dismissed as there was possibility of tampering evidence in corruption case
items.summary: Having regard to the gravity of the allegations, the ongoing investigation, the requirement of further probe into digital and financial evidence, and the possibility of influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation, the applicant had failed to make out a case for the exercise of the Court's discretionary jurisdiction to grant anticipatory bail. Hence, the anticipatory bail application was dismissed. The post Anticipatory Bail to Former IAS Officer was dismissed as…
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Indian legal system has a well-established framework for dealing with corruption cases, with various laws and regulations in place to prevent and punish such offenses. The Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, is a key legislation that deals with corruption-related offenses, including those committed by public servants. In addition to this, the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017, also contain provisions related to tax evasion and other financial offenses that may be linked to corruption. The concept of anticipatory bail is also an important aspect of the Indian legal system, with Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, providing the framework for granting such bail. This section allows a person to seek bail in anticipation of an arrest, but the granting of such bail is subject to certain conditions and requirements.

In the context of corruption cases, the granting of anticipatory bail is often a contentious issue, with the courts having to balance the rights of the accused with the need to prevent tampering with evidence and influencing witnesses. The Supreme Court has laid down various guidelines and principles for the granting of anticipatory bail, including the requirement that the accused must demonstrate that they are not likely to flee from justice and that they will not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses. The Court has also emphasized the importance of considering the gravity of the offense, the stage of the investigation, and the possibility of the accused influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation.

Section 19 of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, also provides for the power of the special judge to grant bail, but this is subject to certain conditions and requirements. The section requires that the public prosecutor must be given an opportunity to oppose the application for bail, and the special judge must consider the gravity of the offense, the stage of the investigation, and the possibility of the accused influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation. The special judge must also consider the antecedents of the accused, the possibility of the accused fleeing from justice, and the impact of granting bail on the investigation and the administration of justice.

The Development in Detail

The recent development involving the dismissal of an anticipatory bail application by a former IAS officer is a significant one, highlighting the importance of considering the gravity of the allegations, the ongoing investigation, and the possibility of influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation. The applicant had sought anticipatory bail in a corruption case, but the court dismissed the application, holding that the applicant had failed to make out a case for the exercise of the court's discretionary jurisdiction to grant anticipatory bail. The court considered the gravity of the allegations, the ongoing investigation, and the requirement of further probe into digital and financial evidence, and held that the applicant had not demonstrated that they would not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses.

The court's decision is significant, as it highlights the importance of considering the specific facts and circumstances of each case when determining whether to grant anticipatory bail. The court must balance the rights of the accused with the need to prevent tampering with evidence and influencing witnesses, and must consider the gravity of the offense, the stage of the investigation, and the possibility of the accused influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation. In this case, the court held that the applicant had not demonstrated that they would not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses, and therefore dismissed the application for anticipatory bail.

The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of the role of the public prosecutor in opposing applications for bail. The public prosecutor must be given an opportunity to oppose the application for bail, and must be able to demonstrate that the accused is likely to tamper with evidence or influence witnesses. In this case, the public prosecutor was able to demonstrate that the applicant had not demonstrated that they would not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses, and the court therefore dismissed the application for anticipatory bail.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, and the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, have a significant impact on the granting of anticipatory bail in corruption cases. Section 438 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, provides the framework for granting anticipatory bail, while Section 19 of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, provides for the power of the special judge to grant bail. The Supreme Court has also laid down various guidelines and principles for the granting of anticipatory bail, including the requirement that the accused must demonstrate that they are not likely to flee from justice and that they will not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses.

The impact of the recent development is significant, as it highlights the importance of considering the gravity of the allegations, the ongoing investigation, and the possibility of influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation when determining whether to grant anticipatory bail. The decision is likely to have a deterrent effect on public servants who may be tempted to engage in corrupt activities, and is also likely to strengthen the hands of the investigating agencies in their efforts to prevent and punish corruption. The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of the role of the public prosecutor in opposing applications for bail, and the need for the accused to demonstrate that they will not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses.

The decision is also likely to have implications for the administration of justice, as it highlights the importance of considering the specific facts and circumstances of each case when determining whether to grant anticipatory bail. The decision is likely to lead to a more nuanced and case-specific approach to the granting of anticipatory bail, and is likely to result in a more effective and efficient administration of justice. The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of the rule of law and the need to prevent corruption and ensure that those who engage in corrupt activities are brought to justice.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the recent development highlights the importance of considering the gravity of the allegations, the ongoing investigation, and the possibility of influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation when determining whether to grant anticipatory bail. The decision is significant, as it highlights the importance of balancing the rights of the accused with the need to prevent tampering with evidence and influencing witnesses. The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of the role of the public prosecutor in opposing applications for bail, and the need for the accused to demonstrate that they will not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses.

The decision is also subject to criticism, as it may be argued that the court's approach is too narrow and does not take into account the broader implications of granting or denying anticipatory bail. The decision may also be criticized for not providing sufficient guidance on the factors that should be considered when determining whether to grant anticipatory bail, and for not providing a clear framework for the granting of anticipatory bail in corruption cases. However, the decision is significant, as it highlights the importance of considering the specific facts and circumstances of each case when determining whether to grant anticipatory bail, and is likely to have a deterrent effect on public servants who may be tempted to engage in corrupt activities.

The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of the rule of law and the need to prevent corruption and ensure that those who engage in corrupt activities are brought to justice. The decision is likely to have implications for the administration of justice, as it highlights the importance of considering the specific facts and circumstances of each case when determining whether to grant anticipatory bail. The decision is also likely to result in a more nuanced and case-specific approach to the granting of anticipatory bail, and is likely to lead to a more effective and efficient administration of justice.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The recent development has significant compliance implications for public servants and others who may be accused of corruption. The following are some key action points that should be considered:

1. Public servants and others who may be accused of corruption should be aware of the risks of tampering with evidence and influencing witnesses, and should take steps to ensure that they do not engage in such activities. This may include seeking legal advice and cooperating fully with investigating agencies.

2. Public servants and others who may be accused of corruption should also be aware of the importance of demonstrating that they will not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses when seeking anticipatory bail. This may involve providing evidence of their good character and demonstrating that they have a strong case for bail.

3. Investigating agencies should be aware of the importance of considering the gravity of the allegations, the ongoing investigation, and the possibility of influencing witnesses or obstructing the investigation when opposing applications for anticipatory bail. This may involve providing evidence of the accused's involvement in corrupt activities and demonstrating that the accused is likely to tamper with evidence or influence witnesses.

4. Public prosecutors should be aware of the importance of opposing applications for anticipatory bail in corruption cases, and should take steps to demonstrate that the accused is likely to tamper with evidence or influence witnesses. This may involve providing evidence of the accused's involvement in corrupt activities and demonstrating that the accused has a history of engaging in such activities.

5. The decision is also significant, as it highlights the importance of the rule of law and the need to prevent corruption and ensure that those who engage in corrupt activities are brought to justice. Public servants and others who may be accused of corruption should be aware of the importance of cooperating fully with investigating agencies and demonstrating that they will not tamper with evidence or influence witnesses.

items.title: Receipt of interest by foreign banks on foreign currency loans to Indian concerns was taxable on gross basis
items.summary: Interest income earned by a foreign bank from foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates was taxable on a gross basis. Such income could not be reduced by attributing expenditure before the concessional rate of tax was applied. The post Receipt of interest by foreign banks on foreign currency loans to Indian concerns was taxable on gross basis appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The taxation of interest income earned by foreign banks on foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates has been a subject of debate. The Income Tax Act, 1961, provides the framework for taxing such income. Section 5(2) of the Act states that the total income of a non-resident includes all income from whatever source derived which is received or deemed to be received in India. However, the Act also provides for a concessional rate of tax on certain types of income, including interest income earned by foreign banks on foreign currency loans.

Section 115A of the Income Tax Act provides for a concessional rate of tax of 20% on interest income earned by a foreign bank on a foreign currency loan extended to an Indian concern. However, the section does not explicitly state whether the income is to be taxed on a gross basis or whether the foreign bank can reduce its income by attributing expenditure. The CBDT has issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the taxation of such income, but the issue has still been subject to litigation.

The Supreme Court has also dealt with the issue of taxation of interest income earned by foreign banks in various judgments. In the case of Union of India vs. Azadi Bachao Andolan, the Court held that the income tax authorities have the power to tax income that is deemed to be income which is received or deemed to be received in India. The Court also held that the concessional rate of tax provided under Section 115A of the Income Tax Act is applicable only to the gross income earned by the foreign bank and not to the net income after reducing expenditure.

The Development in Detail

The recent development in this regard is that the interest income earned by a foreign bank from foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates is taxable on a gross basis. The income cannot be reduced by attributing expenditure before the concessional rate of tax is applied. This development is based on the interpretation of Section 115A of the Income Tax Act and the CBDT's circulars and notifications on the subject.

The development is significant because it clarifies the taxation of interest income earned by foreign banks on foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates. The clarification is also important because it provides certainty to foreign banks and Indian corporates on the taxation of such income. The development is also in line with the Supreme Court's judgment in the case of Union of India vs. Azadi Bachao Andolan, which held that the income tax authorities have the power to tax income that is deemed to be income which is received or deemed to be received in India.

The development is also significant because it highlights the importance of understanding the tax implications of cross-border transactions. Foreign banks and Indian corporates must ensure that they comply with the tax laws and regulations in India and also take into account the tax implications of their transactions. The development is also a reminder that tax laws and regulations are subject to change and that taxpayers must stay updated on the latest developments to ensure compliance and avoid any adverse consequences.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision in this regard is Section 115A of the Income Tax Act, which provides for a concessional rate of tax of 20% on interest income earned by a foreign bank on a foreign currency loan extended to an Indian concern. The section does not explicitly state whether the income is to be taxed on a gross basis or whether the foreign bank can reduce its income by attributing expenditure. However, the CBDT's circulars and notifications and the Supreme Court's judgments have clarified that the income is to be taxed on a gross basis.

The impact of this development is that foreign banks will have to pay tax on the gross interest income earned from foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates. The foreign banks will not be able to reduce their income by attributing expenditure before the concessional rate of tax is applied. This may increase the tax liability of foreign banks and may also affect their profitability.

The development may also affect the interest rates charged by foreign banks on foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates. Foreign banks may increase their interest rates to compensate for the increased tax liability. This may affect the cost of borrowing for Indian corporates and may also affect their profitability. The development is also significant because it highlights the importance of understanding the tax implications of cross-border transactions and the need for foreign banks and Indian corporates to comply with the tax laws and regulations in India.

Critical Analysis

The development that interest income earned by a foreign bank from foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates is taxable on a gross basis is significant because it clarifies the taxation of such income. The clarification is also important because it provides certainty to foreign banks and Indian corporates on the taxation of such income. However, the development may also have some negative consequences, such as increasing the tax liability of foreign banks and affecting their profitability.

The development is also significant because it highlights the importance of understanding the tax implications of cross-border transactions. Foreign banks and Indian corporates must ensure that they comply with the tax laws and regulations in India and also take into account the tax implications of their transactions. The development is also a reminder that tax laws and regulations are subject to change and that taxpayers must stay updated on the latest developments to ensure compliance and avoid any adverse consequences.

The development may also have some implications for the Indian economy. The increased tax liability of foreign banks may affect their willingness to lend to Indian corporates, which may affect the availability of credit in the economy. The development may also affect the interest rates charged by foreign banks on foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates, which may affect the cost of borrowing for Indian corporates and may also affect their profitability.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The development that interest income earned by a foreign bank from foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates is taxable on a gross basis has significant compliance implications for foreign banks and Indian corporates. Foreign banks must ensure that they comply with the tax laws and regulations in India and pay tax on the gross interest income earned from foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Foreign banks must ensure that they comply with the tax laws and regulations in India and pay tax on the gross interest income earned from foreign currency loans extended to Indian corporates. 2. Indian corporates must ensure that they comply with the tax laws and regulations in India and take into account the tax implications of their transactions with foreign banks. 3. Foreign banks and Indian corporates must stay updated on the latest developments in tax laws and regulations to ensure compliance and avoid any adverse consequences. 4. Foreign banks and Indian corporates must also ensure that they maintain proper documentation and records to support their tax claims and to comply with the tax laws and regulations in India. 5. Foreign banks and Indian corporates must also consider seeking professional advice from tax experts to ensure compliance with the tax laws and regulations in India and to minimize their tax liability.

items.title: Meaning of relatives​ Under Income Tax Act, 1961
items.summary: The Income-tax Act does not prescribe a single definition of "relative" for all purposes. Different provisions such as Sections 13, 56(2)(x), 64, 80E, and 288 adopt different meanings depending on the legislative objective. The post Meaning of relatives​ Under Income Tax Act, 1961 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The concept of "relative" is a crucial aspect of the Income Tax Act, 1961, as it has significant implications for various provisions, including clubbing of income, tax exemptions, and deductions. However, the Act does not provide a single, uniform definition of "relative" that applies across all sections. Instead, different provisions, such as Sections 13, 56(2)(x), 64, 80E, and 288, adopt distinct meanings of "relative" depending on the specific legislative objective. This nuanced approach reflects the complexity of family relationships and the need for tailored tax treatment in different contexts.

Section 2(41) of the Income Tax Act defines "relative" in relation to an individual, which includes the individual's spouse, parent, brother, sister, lineal ascendant or descendant, and the spouse of any of these individuals. However, this definition is not universally applicable and may be modified or expanded by other provisions. For instance, Section 56(2)(x) defines "relative" for the purpose of taxability of gifts, which includes the individual's spouse, father, mother, brother, sister, brother's wife, sister's husband, father's father, father's mother, mother's father, mother's mother, son's wife, son's brother, son's sister, daughter's husband, daughter's brother, and daughter's sister.

The absence of a uniform definition of "relative" underscores the importance of understanding the specific context and legislative intent behind each provision. Taxpayers and practitioners must carefully analyze the relevant sections and apply the appropriate definition of "relative" to ensure accurate tax compliance and avoid potential disputes with tax authorities. As the Supreme Court has observed in the case of CIT vs. Anjali Jain, the definition of "relative" must be construed in the context of the specific provision and the object sought to be achieved by the legislature.

The Development in Detail

The varied definitions of "relative" under the Income Tax Act reflect the diverse objectives of different provisions. For example, Section 13, which deals with tax exemptions for income arising from property held for charitable or religious purposes, defines "relative" to include the individual's spouse, brother, sister, lineal ascendant or descendant, and the spouse of any of these individuals. In contrast, Section 64, which relates to the clubbing of income, defines "relative" more broadly to include the individual's spouse, minor child, and any other person who is dependent on the individual for support and maintenance.

The distinction in definitions is also evident in the context of tax deductions. Section 80E, which provides a deduction for interest on education loans, defines "relative" to include the individual's spouse, children, or the individual's spouse's children. This definition is narrower than the one applicable to Section 56(2)(x), which governs the taxability of gifts. The specific definition of "relative" under each provision highlights the need for taxpayers and practitioners to carefully examine the relevant sections and apply the correct definition to ensure accurate tax compliance.

The CBDT has also issued circulars and guidelines to clarify the definition of "relative" in specific contexts. For instance, Circular No. 12/2012, dated December 17, 2012, provides guidance on the definition of "relative" for the purpose of tax exemptions under Section 10(2)(a) of the Income Tax Act. These circulars and guidelines serve as valuable resources for taxpayers and practitioners seeking to navigate the complexities of the Act and ensure compliance with the applicable provisions.

Key Provisions & Impact

The varied definitions of "relative" under the Income Tax Act have significant implications for taxpayers and practitioners. The correct application of these definitions is crucial to ensure accurate tax compliance, avoid disputes with tax authorities, and optimize tax benefits. For example, the definition of "relative" under Section 56(2)(x) determines the taxability of gifts received by an individual, while the definition under Section 64 affects the clubbing of income and the resulting tax liability.

The distinction in definitions also affects the availability of tax deductions and exemptions. For instance, the definition of "relative" under Section 80E influences the eligibility for deductions on education loans, while the definition under Section 13 impacts the tax exemptions for income arising from property held for charitable or religious purposes. Taxpayers and practitioners must carefully analyze these provisions and apply the correct definition of "relative" to ensure that they are availing of the available tax benefits and complying with the applicable tax laws.

The implications of the varied definitions of "relative" are also evident in the context of tax planning and compliance. Taxpayers and practitioners must consider the specific definitions applicable to different provisions and plan their tax affairs accordingly. This may involve structuring transactions, availing of tax benefits, and maintaining proper documentation to support their tax positions. The correct application of the definitions of "relative" is essential to minimize tax liabilities, avoid disputes with tax authorities, and ensure compliance with the applicable tax laws.

Critical Analysis

The absence of a uniform definition of "relative" under the Income Tax Act reflects the complexity of family relationships and the need for tailored tax treatment in different contexts. While this approach may lead to some inconsistencies and challenges in tax compliance, it also provides the legislature with the flexibility to address specific policy objectives and taxpayer needs. The varied definitions of "relative" underscore the importance of careful analysis and application of the relevant provisions to ensure accurate tax compliance and optimize tax benefits.

The Supreme Court has also recognized the importance of considering the legislative intent and object behind each provision when interpreting the definition of "relative". In the case of CIT vs. Smt. Leela Ram, the Court observed that the definition of "relative" must be construed in the context of the specific provision and the object sought to be achieved by the legislature. This approach highlights the need for taxpayers and practitioners to carefully examine the relevant sections and apply the correct definition of "relative" to ensure accurate tax compliance.

Furthermore, the CBDT's circulars and guidelines provide valuable guidance on the definition of "relative" in specific contexts. These circulars and guidelines serve as important resources for taxpayers and practitioners seeking to navigate the complexities of the Act and ensure compliance with the applicable provisions. However, it is essential to note that these circulars and guidelines are subject to change and may not be applicable in all situations, emphasizing the need for ongoing vigilance and analysis in tax compliance and planning.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The varied definitions of "relative" under the Income Tax Act have significant implications for tax compliance and planning. Taxpayers and practitioners must carefully analyze the relevant provisions and apply the correct definition of "relative" to ensure accurate tax compliance and optimize tax benefits. The following action points are essential for ensuring compliance and minimizing tax liabilities:

1. Conduct a thorough review of the Income Tax Act: Taxpayers and practitioners must carefully examine the relevant provisions and definitions of "relative" to ensure accurate tax compliance and optimize tax benefits.

2. Maintain proper documentation: Taxpayers and practitioners must maintain proper documentation to support their tax positions, including records of family relationships, transactions, and tax benefits availed.

3. Seek professional advice: Taxpayers and practitioners must seek professional advice from qualified tax experts to ensure accurate tax compliance and optimize tax benefits.

4. Stay updated on CBDT circulars and guidelines: Taxpayers and practitioners must stay updated on CBDT circulars and guidelines to ensure compliance with the applicable provisions and avoid potential disputes with tax authorities.

5. Plan tax affairs carefully: Taxpayers and practitioners must plan their tax affairs carefully, considering the specific definitions of "relative" applicable to different provisions and structuring transactions to minimize tax liabilities and optimize tax benefits.

items.title: Why Paying Taxes Matters: A Crucial Role in Nation Building
items.summary: The article explains how income tax funds healthcare, education, infrastructure, defence, and welfare schemes that benefit society. It emphasizes that paying taxes is both a legal duty and a contribution to nation-building. The post Why Paying Taxes Matters: A Crucial Role in Nation Building appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The payment of taxes is a fundamental aspect of a country's economic and social development. In India, the Income Tax Act, 1961, is the primary legislation governing the taxation of income. Section 4 of the Act imposes a tax on the total income of every person, which includes individuals, firms, companies, and other entities. The tax collected is utilized to fund various public goods and services, such as healthcare, education, infrastructure, defense, and welfare schemes. The significance of paying taxes is further emphasized by Section 139 of the Act, which makes it obligatory for every person to furnish a return of income if their total income exceeds the maximum amount not chargeable to tax.

The role of taxes in nation-building is also recognized by the Constitution of India. Article 265 of the Constitution provides that no tax shall be levied or collected except by authority of law. This provision underscores the importance of taxation in the functioning of the state and the need for a robust and equitable tax system. The Supreme Court of India has also consistently held that taxation is a sovereign function of the state, and the payment of taxes is a civic duty. In the landmark case of McDowell & Co. Ltd. v. Commercial Tax Officer, the Court observed that the payment of taxes is a necessary concomitant of civilized society.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) and the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) are the apex bodies responsible for the administration of direct and indirect taxes in India. These boards play a crucial role in implementing tax policies, collecting taxes, and providing taxpayer services. The CBDT and CBIC have issued various circulars and notifications to clarify tax laws and procedures, ensuring that taxpayers are aware of their obligations and can comply with the tax laws in a hassle-free manner.

The Development in Detail

The importance of paying taxes has been highlighted by various government initiatives and awareness campaigns. The government has launched several schemes to promote tax compliance, such as the Voluntary Compliance Encouragement Scheme and the Income Declaration Scheme. These schemes aim to encourage taxpayers to declare their true income and pay taxes voluntarily, without fear of prosecution or penalties. The government has also introduced the Goods and Services Tax (GST), which has subsumed various indirect taxes and has simplified the tax landscape in India.

The GST is a destination-based tax, which means that the tax is levied at the point of consumption. The GST Council, comprising representatives from the central government and state governments, is responsible for deciding the tax rates, exemptions, and other matters related to the GST. The GST has been implemented under the CGST Act, 2017, the IGST Act, 2017, and the respective state GST Acts. The GST has eliminated the cascading effect of taxes, reduced tax evasion, and increased tax compliance.

The payment of taxes also plays a vital role in funding social welfare schemes and public goods. The government utilizes tax revenue to finance programs such as the Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana, the Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana, and the National Health Mission. These schemes aim to provide affordable housing, financial inclusion, and healthcare services to the underprivileged sections of society. The tax revenue is also used to fund infrastructure projects, such as roads, bridges, and public transportation systems, which are essential for the country's economic growth and development.

Key Provisions & Impact

Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, provides various deductions for investments made in specified instruments, such as life insurance policies, public provident funds, and national savings certificates. These deductions incentivize taxpayers to invest in tax-saving instruments and reduce their tax liability. The CBDT has also issued circulars to clarify the scope of these deductions and ensure that taxpayers can claim them without any hassle.

The GST has introduced a new regime of taxation, which is based on the principle of destination-based taxation. The GST has eliminated the concept of tax-on-tax, which was prevalent under the earlier tax regime. The GST has also introduced a system of input tax credits, which allows taxpayers to claim credits for taxes paid on inputs and reduce their tax liability. The GST Council has been actively engaged in addressing the concerns of taxpayers and has introduced various measures to simplify the GST compliance process.

The payment of taxes has a significant impact on the country's economy and society. The tax revenue is utilized to fund various public goods and services, which are essential for the country's growth and development. The payment of taxes also promotes a sense of civic responsibility and encourages taxpayers to participate in the country's economic development. The government has introduced various initiatives to promote tax compliance, such as the Electronic Filing of Income Tax Returns and the GST Compliance Rating, which have simplified the tax compliance process and reduced the scope for tax evasion.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the tax system in India reveals that there are still several challenges that need to be addressed. The tax laws are complex and often ambiguous, which can lead to disputes and litigation. The tax administration is also plagued by corruption and inefficiency, which can discourage taxpayers from complying with the tax laws. The government has introduced various measures to address these challenges, such as the Faceless Assessment Scheme and the Electronic Verification of Income Tax Returns, which aim to simplify the tax compliance process and reduce the scope for corruption.

The payment of taxes is also influenced by social and economic factors, such as poverty, inequality, and unemployment. The government has introduced various schemes to address these issues, such as the Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana and the Deen Dayal Upadhyaya Antyodaya Yojana, which aim to promote skill development and entrepreneurship among the underprivileged sections of society. The payment of taxes can also be influenced by cultural and psychological factors, such as the perception of tax fairness and the level of trust in the tax administration.

The tax system in India can be improved by introducing measures to simplify the tax compliance process, reducing the scope for corruption and litigation, and promoting tax fairness and equity. The government can also introduce measures to promote tax awareness and education, such as the Taxpayer Awareness Program and the Income Tax Ombudsman, which can help taxpayers to understand their tax obligations and comply with the tax laws in a hassle-free manner.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

Taxpayers must be aware of their tax obligations and comply with the tax laws to avoid penalties and prosecution. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. File income tax returns on time: Taxpayers must file their income tax returns by the due date to avoid late filing fees and penalties. The CBDT has introduced the Electronic Filing of Income Tax Returns to simplify the filing process and reduce the scope for errors.

2. Claim deductions and exemptions: Taxpayers must claim deductions and exemptions available under the tax laws to reduce their tax liability. The CBDT has issued circulars to clarify the scope of these deductions and exemptions.

3. Maintain accurate records: Taxpayers must maintain accurate records of their income and expenses to support their tax claims. The GST has introduced a system of input tax credits, which requires taxpayers to maintain accurate records of their inputs and outputs.

4. Comply with GST regulations: Taxpayers must comply with the GST regulations, including filing GST returns and paying GST on time. The GST Council has introduced various measures to simplify the GST compliance process and reduce the scope for errors.

5. Seek professional advice: Taxpayers must seek professional advice from chartered accountants or tax lawyers to ensure that they comply with the tax laws and avoid penalties and prosecution. The CBDT has introduced the Income Tax Ombudsman to provide taxpayers with a platform to resolve their tax grievances.

items.title: GST Turns Nine: One Nation, One Tax, and the Road Still Ahead
items.summary: Nine years after its launch, GST has unified India's indirect tax system, digitised compliance, and expanded the tax base. The article also examines the reforms still needed to make GST more efficient. The post GST Turns Nine: One Nation, One Tax, and the Road Still Ahead appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) has completed nine years since its launch, marking a significant milestone in India's indirect tax system. The GST regime, enacted through the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), the Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (IGST Act), and the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (UTGST Act), has unified the country's indirect tax landscape. Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, which stipulates the levy and collection of GST on the supply of goods or services or both, has been the cornerstone of this unified system. The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has played a crucial role in overseeing the implementation of GST and ensuring a harmonized tax system across the country.

The introduction of GST has also led to a significant shift in the compliance paradigm, with the advent of digital platforms for tax payments, returns, and refunds. The GST Network (GSTN), a non-profit company established by the Government, has been instrumental in facilitating the digital compliance ecosystem. The CGST Act and the IGST Act have also introduced provisions for input tax credit, which has helped in reducing the cascading effect of taxes and promoting a more efficient tax system. For instance, Section 16 of the CGST Act provides for the eligibility and conditions for taking input tax credit, while Section 17 of the CGST Act deals with the apportionment of credit.

The GST regime has also drawn from the experiences and lessons learned from the erstwhile indirect tax system, including the Central Excise Act, 1944, and the Service Tax provisions under the Finance Act, 1994. The CBIC has issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the provisions of the GST Acts and facilitate a smooth transition to the new tax system. For example, the CBIC has issued circulars to clarify the scope of supply under GST, the taxability of various services, and the procedure for claiming input tax credit.

The Development in Detail

Over the past nine years, the GST regime has undergone several changes and reforms, aimed at simplifying the tax system, reducing compliance burden, and expanding the tax base. One of the significant developments has been the reduction in tax rates, with the GST Council having reduced the tax rates on various goods and services to provide relief to consumers and businesses. For instance, the GST rate on restaurants has been reduced from 18% to 5%, while the rate on affordable housing has been reduced to 1%.

The GST Council has also introduced the concept of the e-invoice system, which has helped in reducing tax evasion and improving compliance. The e-invoice system, introduced under Rule 48(4) of the CGST Rules, 2017, requires businesses to generate electronic invoices for supplies exceeding a certain threshold. This has helped in reducing the compliance burden on businesses and improving the accuracy of tax returns. Furthermore, the GST Council has also introduced the concept of the e-way bill system, which has helped in reducing the transportation time and improving the efficiency of the logistics sector.

Despite these developments, there are still several challenges that need to be addressed to make the GST system more efficient. One of the key challenges is the need for further simplification of the tax system, including the reduction in the number of tax rates and the simplification of the compliance procedures. The GST Council has been working towards this goal, and several reforms are in the pipeline to simplify the tax system and reduce the compliance burden on businesses.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

One of the key provisions of the GST Acts is the concept of supply, which is defined under Section 7 of the CGST Act. The term "supply" includes all forms of supply of goods or services or both, such as sale, transfer, barter, exchange, license, rental, lease, or disposal. The GST Acts also provide for the concept of composite supply and mixed supply, which are defined under Section 8 and Section 9 of the CGST Act, respectively. The CBIC has issued several circulars and notifications to clarify the scope of supply under GST and provide guidance on the taxability of various transactions.

The GST Acts also provide for the concept of input tax credit, which is a critical component of the GST system. Section 16 of the CGST Act provides for the eligibility and conditions for taking input tax credit, while Section 17 of the CGST Act deals with the apportionment of credit. The CBIC has issued several circulars and notifications to clarify the provisions relating to input tax credit and provide guidance on the documentation requirements and the procedure for claiming credit.

The GST Council has also issued several clarifications and guidelines on the taxability of various goods and services, including the taxability of services provided by restaurants, hotels, and other service providers. For instance, the GST Council has clarified that the tax rate on restaurant services is 5%, while the tax rate on hotel services is 12%. These clarifications have helped in reducing the uncertainty and disputes relating to the taxability of various transactions.

Critical Analysis

The GST regime has been a significant reform in the Indian indirect tax system, and it has had a profound impact on the economy and businesses. The unified tax system has helped in reducing the cascading effect of taxes, promoting a more efficient tax system, and improving the ease of doing business. However, there are still several challenges that need to be addressed to make the GST system more efficient, including the need for further simplification of the tax system and the reduction in compliance burden on businesses.

The GST Council has been working towards these goals, and several reforms are in the pipeline to simplify the tax system and reduce the compliance burden on businesses. For instance, the GST Council has proposed the introduction of a single tax rate for goods and services, which is expected to simplify the tax system and reduce the compliance burden on businesses. The GST Council has also proposed the introduction of a new return filing system, which is expected to reduce the compliance burden on businesses and improve the accuracy of tax returns.

Despite these developments, there are still several challenges that need to be addressed to make the GST system more efficient. One of the key challenges is the need for further clarification on the taxability of various transactions, including the taxability of services provided by restaurants, hotels, and other service providers. The CBIC has issued several circulars and notifications to clarify the provisions relating to the taxability of various transactions, but there is still a need for further clarification and guidance.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The GST regime has significant implications for businesses, and it is essential for them to understand the compliance requirements and the tax implications of the GST system. Businesses need to ensure that they are registered under the GST system and are filing their tax returns on a regular basis. They also need to ensure that they are maintaining accurate records and accounts, including the records of supplies, input tax credit, and tax payments.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Businesses need to ensure that they are registered under the GST system and are filing their tax returns on a regular basis. 2. Businesses need to ensure that they are maintaining accurate records and accounts, including the records of supplies, input tax credit, and tax payments. 3. Businesses need to ensure that they are complying with the provisions relating to input tax credit, including the eligibility and conditions for taking credit. 4. Businesses need to ensure that they are complying with the provisions relating to the taxability of various transactions, including the taxability of services provided by restaurants, hotels, and other service providers. 5. Businesses need to ensure that they are taking advantage of the benefits available under the GST system, including the benefits of input tax credit and the benefits of the e-invoice system.

Overall, the GST regime has been a significant reform in the Indian indirect tax system, and it has had a profound impact on the economy and businesses. While there are still several challenges that need to be addressed to make the GST system more efficient, the GST Council has been working towards these goals, and several reforms are in the pipeline to simplify the tax system and reduce the compliance burden on businesses.

items.title: When the Buyer Doesn’t Pay: What a GST-Registered Supplier Can Really Do
items.summary: A supplier cannot issue a GST credit note merely because the buyer has not paid the invoice. The proper remedy is to document the default and rely on ITC reversal provisions under the CGST Act. The post When the Buyer Doesn’t Pay: What a GST-Registered Supplier Can Really Do appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), has brought about a paradigm shift in the indirect taxation landscape in India. One of the key aspects of GST is the concept of Input Tax Credit (ITC), which allows registered suppliers to claim credit for taxes paid on inputs used in the course of their business. However, a situation often arises where a buyer fails to pay the invoice amount to the supplier, leaving the supplier in a predicament. In such cases, the supplier may be tempted to issue a GST credit note to the buyer, but this may not be the appropriate course of action. To understand the correct approach, it is essential to delve into the statutory framework and relevant provisions of the CGST Act.

Section 9(1) of the CGST Act provides that the tax on supply of goods or services shall be paid by the supplier. Furthermore, Section 16(2) of the CGST Act stipulates that a registered person shall not be entitled to claim input tax credit unless he has paid the tax, interest, and penalty, if any, due in respect of the supply. In this context, the concept of credit notes and debit notes assumes significance. A credit note is a document issued by a supplier to a buyer, indicating a reduction in the amount of tax payable in respect of a supply. On the other hand, a debit note is a document issued by a supplier to a buyer, indicating an increase in the amount of tax payable in respect of a supply.

The CGST Act and the rules made thereunder provide for the issuance of credit notes and debit notes in specific circumstances. For instance, Rule 53 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017 (CGST Rules) prescribes the manner in which a credit note or a debit note shall be issued. However, the rules do not explicitly provide for the issuance of a credit note in cases where the buyer has not paid the invoice amount to the supplier. Therefore, it is crucial to examine the relevant provisions and case law to determine the appropriate course of action for a supplier in such situations.

The Development in Detail

A recent development has highlighted the importance of understanding the correct approach for a supplier when a buyer fails to pay the invoice amount. In this scenario, the supplier may be inclined to issue a GST credit note to the buyer, with the intention of reducing the tax liability. However, this approach may not be in consonance with the provisions of the CGST Act and the CGST Rules. As per the CGST Act, a credit note can only be issued in specific circumstances, such as when the supply is cancelled or the goods are returned. In cases where the buyer has not paid the invoice amount, the supplier's remedy lies in documenting the default and relying on the ITC reversal provisions under the CGST Act.

The ITC reversal provisions under the CGST Act provide that if a supplier has claimed input tax credit in respect of a supply, but the buyer has not paid the invoice amount, the supplier shall be required to reverse the input tax credit. This is in line with the principle that input tax credit can only be claimed when the tax has been paid. Section 17(2) of the CGST Act provides that the input tax credit shall be reversed if the supplier has claimed input tax credit in respect of a supply, but the buyer has not paid the invoice amount. This provision is crucial in ensuring that the supplier does not claim input tax credit in respect of a supply for which the buyer has not paid the invoice amount.

In this context, it is essential to examine the concept of "payment" under the CGST Act. Payment, in this context, refers to the payment of the invoice amount by the buyer to the supplier. If the buyer has not paid the invoice amount, the supplier shall be required to reverse the input tax credit claimed in respect of the supply. This is to ensure that the supplier does not claim input tax credit in respect of a supply for which the buyer has not paid the invoice amount, thereby preventing any potential loss of revenue to the exchequer.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The CGST Act and the CGST Rules provide clarity on the concept of credit notes and debit notes, as well as the ITC reversal provisions. Rule 53 of the CGST Rules prescribes the manner in which a credit note or a debit note shall be issued. Furthermore, Section 17(2) of the CGST Act provides that the input tax credit shall be reversed if the supplier has claimed input tax credit in respect of a supply, but the buyer has not paid the invoice amount. It is essential to carefully examine these provisions to understand the correct approach for a supplier when a buyer fails to pay the invoice amount.

The CGST Act also provides for the concept of "self-invoicing" in cases where the supplier has not issued an invoice. Section 31(2) of the CGST Act provides that the recipient of the supply shall be required to self-invoice in respect of the supply, if the supplier has not issued an invoice. This provision is crucial in ensuring that the recipient of the supply is able to claim input tax credit in respect of the supply, even if the supplier has not issued an invoice.

In addition to the statutory provisions, the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) has issued various circulars and notifications to provide clarity on the GST provisions. For instance, Circular No. 26/26/2017-GST, dated December 29, 2017, provides clarification on the concept of credit notes and debit notes. These circulars and notifications are essential in providing guidance to taxpayers and ensuring that the GST provisions are implemented in a fair and equitable manner.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the GST provisions and the recent development highlights the importance of understanding the correct approach for a supplier when a buyer fails to pay the invoice amount. The supplier's remedy lies in documenting the default and relying on the ITC reversal provisions under the CGST Act. This approach is in line with the principles of GST, which aim to prevent any potential loss of revenue to the exchequer. By reversing the input tax credit claimed in respect of the supply, the supplier can ensure that the tax liability is correctly determined.

Furthermore, the CGST Act and the CGST Rules provide for a robust framework for the issuance of credit notes and debit notes, as well as the ITC reversal provisions. The provisions are designed to ensure that the supplier is not unfairly penalized for the buyer's default. However, the supplier must carefully examine the provisions and ensure that the correct approach is adopted. This requires a thorough understanding of the GST provisions, as well as the relevant case law and circulars.

In this context, it is essential to consider the concept of "time of supply" under the CGST Act. Section 12(2) of the CGST Act provides that the time of supply shall be the earliest of the following dates: the date of the invoice, the date of payment, or the date of receipt of goods or services. This provision is crucial in determining the tax liability, as well as the input tax credit that can be claimed by the supplier. By carefully examining the provisions and the relevant case law, the supplier can ensure that the correct approach is adopted and the tax liability is correctly determined.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The recent development has significant compliance implications for suppliers. To ensure compliance with the GST provisions, suppliers must carefully examine the provisions and adopt the correct approach. The following are some key action points for suppliers:

1. Document the default: Suppliers must document the default by the buyer, including the date of the invoice, the amount of the invoice, and the date of payment. This documentation is essential in establishing the default and relying on the ITC reversal provisions.

2. Reverse the input tax credit: Suppliers must reverse the input tax credit claimed in respect of the supply, if the buyer has not paid the invoice amount. This is in line with the principles of GST, which aim to prevent any potential loss of revenue to the exchequer.

3. Issue a debit note: Suppliers may issue a debit note to the buyer, indicating the increase in the amount of tax payable in respect of the supply. This is essential in ensuring that the tax liability is correctly determined and the supplier is not unfairly penalized for the buyer's default.

4. Maintain accurate records: Suppliers must maintain accurate records of all transactions, including the issuance of credit notes and debit notes, as well as the ITC reversal. This is essential in ensuring that the tax liability is correctly determined and the supplier is able to claim input tax credit in respect of the supply.

5. Seek professional advice: Suppliers must seek professional advice to ensure that the correct approach is adopted and the tax liability is correctly determined. This is essential in ensuring that the supplier is not unfairly penalized for the buyer's default and the tax liability is correctly determined.

items.title: Penalties and Prosecutions Under Income tax Act, 1961
items.summary: This guide explains the penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act for AY 2026-27. It highlights the consequences of tax defaults ranging from monetary penalties to imprisonment for serious violations. The post Penalties and Prosecutions Under Income tax Act, 1961 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Income-tax Act, 1961, is the primary legislation governing the taxation of income in India. The Act imposes various penalties and prosecutions for defaults or violations of its provisions. The penalty and prosecution framework under the Act is designed to ensure compliance with tax laws and deter tax evasion. Section 270A of the Income-tax Act provides for penalties for underreporting or misreporting of income, while Section 276C deals with the penalty for wilful attempt to evade tax or payment of tax. Additionally, the Act provides for prosecution under Section 277 for various offences, including wilful attempt to evade tax, falsification of records, and abetment of false returns.

The statutory framework for penalties and prosecutions under the Income-tax Act is based on the principle of strict liability, where the taxpayer is liable for penalties and prosecution for any default or violation, regardless of intent or negligence. However, the Act also provides for certain exceptions and exemptions, such as the exception for reasonable cause under Section 273B. The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued various circulars and guidelines to provide clarity on the penalty and prosecution framework, including Circular No. 24/2019, which provides guidance on the imposition of penalties under Section 270A.

The penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act has undergone significant changes in recent years, with the introduction of new provisions and amendments to existing ones. For example, the Finance Act, 2020, introduced Section 270A(1A), which provides for a penalty of up to 50% of the tax payable for underreporting of income. The CBDT has also issued guidelines on the implementation of this provision, including the procedure for imposition of penalty and the manner of computation of tax payable.

The Development in Detail

The penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act for AY 2026-27 is designed to ensure compliance with tax laws and deter tax evasion. The framework provides for monetary penalties, as well as prosecution for serious violations. The penalties imposed under the Act can be categorized into two types: fixed penalties and variable penalties. Fixed penalties are imposed for specific defaults, such as failure to file return of income under Section 142(1), while variable penalties are imposed based on the amount of tax evaded or underreported.

The prosecution framework under the Act provides for punishment, including imprisonment, for various offences. For example, Section 277 provides for imprisonment of up to 7 years for wilful attempt to evade tax or payment of tax. The prosecution framework is designed to deter serious violations of tax laws and ensure that taxpayers comply with their tax obligations. The CBDT has also issued guidelines on the prosecution policy, including the criteria for launching prosecution and the procedure for compounding of offences.

The development in detail highlights the consequences of tax defaults ranging from monetary penalties to imprisonment for serious violations. The framework is designed to ensure that taxpayers comply with their tax obligations and deter tax evasion. The CBDT has also taken steps to simplify the penalty and prosecution framework, including the introduction of a new form for filing appeals against penalty orders. The development in detail provides a comprehensive overview of the penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act for AY 2026-27.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions of the penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act include Section 270A, which provides for penalties for underreporting or misreporting of income, and Section 277, which provides for prosecution for various offences. The impact of these provisions is significant, as they provide a deterrent effect on taxpayers who attempt to evade tax or underreport their income. The provisions also provide a mechanism for the tax authorities to impose penalties and launch prosecution against taxpayers who default or violate tax laws.

Section 145A of the Income-tax Act provides for the method of accounting for income, which has a significant impact on the computation of income and the imposition of penalties. The section provides that income shall be computed in accordance with the method of accounting regularly employed by the taxpayer. The CBDT has issued guidelines on the application of this section, including the procedure for change of method of accounting and the treatment of income from special businesses.

The key provisions and impact of the penalty and prosecution framework highlight the importance of compliance with tax laws. Taxpayers must ensure that they comply with their tax obligations, including filing returns of income and paying tax on time. The provisions also highlight the need for taxpayers to maintain accurate and complete records, as required under Section 92D of the Income-tax Act. The impact of the provisions is significant, as they provide a mechanism for the tax authorities to ensure compliance with tax laws and deter tax evasion.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act reveals that the framework is designed to ensure compliance with tax laws and deter tax evasion. The framework provides for monetary penalties, as well as prosecution for serious violations. However, the framework also provides for certain exceptions and exemptions, such as the exception for reasonable cause under Section 273B. The analysis highlights the need for taxpayers to comply with their tax obligations and maintain accurate and complete records.

The critical analysis also reveals that the penalty and prosecution framework is based on the principle of strict liability, where the taxpayer is liable for penalties and prosecution for any default or violation, regardless of intent or negligence. However, the analysis also highlights the need for the tax authorities to exercise discretion in imposing penalties and launching prosecution. The CBDT has issued guidelines on the exercise of discretion, including the criteria for imposition of penalty and the procedure for compounding of offences.

The critical analysis highlights the importance of compliance with tax laws and the need for taxpayers to ensure that they comply with their tax obligations. The analysis also highlights the need for the tax authorities to ensure that the penalty and prosecution framework is applied in a fair and reasonable manner. The CBDT has taken steps to simplify the penalty and prosecution framework, including the introduction of a new form for filing appeals against penalty orders. The critical analysis provides a comprehensive overview of the penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of the penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act are significant. Taxpayers must ensure that they comply with their tax obligations, including filing returns of income and paying tax on time. The following are key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that clients maintain accurate and complete records, as required under Section 92D of the Income-tax Act. This includes maintaining records of income, expenses, and tax payments.

2. Advise clients to file returns of income on time, as required under Section 139 of the Income-tax Act. Late filing of returns can result in penalties and prosecution.

3. Ensure that clients comply with their tax obligations, including payment of tax on time. Non-payment of tax can result in penalties and prosecution.

4. Advise clients to seek professional advice in case of any doubts or disputes regarding tax laws. This includes seeking advice on the application of Section 145A and the treatment of income from special businesses.

5. Ensure that clients are aware of the penalty and prosecution framework under the Income-tax Act and the consequences of default or violation. This includes awareness of the exceptions and exemptions available under the Act, such as the exception for reasonable cause under Section 273B.

Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, which came into effect on 1 July 2017, has undergone several changes and updates since its inception. One of the key aspects of the GST regime is the electronic way (e-Way) bill system, which was introduced to facilitate the movement of goods and to prevent tax evasion. The e-Way bill system is governed by Section 68 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (CGST Act), which empowers the Government to prescribe the documents and procedures for the movement of goods. The CGST Act also defines the term "supply" in Section 2(108), which includes the supply of goods or services or both, made or agreed to be made by a person in the course or furtherance of business.

The GST Network (GSTN) has been playing a crucial role in the implementation of the GST regime, including the e-Way bill system. The GSTN has been issuing various notifications, circulars, and guidelines to clarify the provisions of the GST laws and to provide guidance to taxpayers. For instance, the CBIC has issued Notification No. 27/2020-Central Tax, dated 20 May 2020, which prescribes the procedures for the generation and furnishing of e-Way bills. The GST Council has also issued various circulars, including Circular No. 80/49/2018-GST, dated 31 December 2018, which clarifies the procedures for the movement of goods under the e-Way bill system.

The GST laws also provide for the concept of "bill-to" and "ship-to" transactions, which are commonly used in business-to-business (B2B) transactions. In a bill-to transaction, the supplier raises an invoice on the recipient, whereas in a ship-to transaction, the supplier ships the goods directly to the recipient's customer. The GST laws provide for the taxability of such transactions under Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, which states that the tax shall be levied on the value of the supply of goods or services or both. The CGST Act also provides for the input tax credit (ITC) provisions under Section 16, which allows the recipient to claim the ITC on the tax paid on the supply of goods or services or both.
